content.text: (Large Language Model Operations) is the set of practices, tooling, and processes for deploying, monitoring, and maintaining LLMs in production. It extends the ideas of MLOps — model versioning, CI/CD pipelines, data validation — to the specifics of language models: prompt management, token-level cost tracking, inference scaling, and continuous evaluation against benchmark suites.

is the operational discipline that sits one level higher. An agent executes multi-step plans, calls external tools, maintains memory across turns, and makes decisions based on intermediate results. AgentOps adds observability and control for these higher-order behaviors: tracing reasoning loops, attributing cost to individual agent runs, auditing tool calls, managing agent state, and enforcing guardrails that can interrupt or redirect an agent mid-execution.

The relationship is additive: every agent system needs LLMOps (you still serve, monitor, and evaluate the underlying model), but agents require AgentOps on top because a single LLM call metric tells you very little about why a 17-step agent run failed at step 9.

LLM applications that ship as simple one-shot request-response systems are relatively straightforward to operate: you log the request, the response, the latency, and the token cost. Agents break this assumption completely.
Multi-step failures are hard to diagnose.

A failed agent run may have succeeded on steps 1 through 8 and failed on step 9. Without per-step tracing you cannot tell which tool call, which LLM invocation, or which decision branch caused the failure.

An agent that loops unexpectedly — calling the LLM 40 times instead of 4 — incurs 10 times the projected cost. LLMOps request-level cost metrics do not surface this; AgentOps run-level cost attribution does.
Tool side-effects require an audit trail.

If an agent sends an email, writes to a database, or calls an external API, operations teams need to know exactly what was called, with what arguments, and by which agent run — not just that an LLM call happened.

A prompt regression that subtly degrades single-call quality becomes a catastrophic failure when the same model is called 15 times in a reasoning loop. AgentOps evaluation pipelines catch regressions at the task level before they reach production.
In healthcare, finance, and legal domains, decision trails must be auditable at the action level. AgentOps provides the structured logs needed to satisfy these requirements.
Step-level traces pinpoint exactly where an agent run deviated — wrong tool, stale memory, or a model hallucination at a specific reasoning step.
Attribute token spend and latency to individual agent sessions, tasks, or users, not just to API endpoints.
Record the full execution trace of a run so you can replay it as a regression test when you change the prompt, model, or tool set.
Safety enforcement at the action layer.
Guardrails that inspect tool arguments before execution prevent destructive actions from completing, even if the LLM generates them.
When agents spawn subagents, AgentOps traces the full call tree so you can see which parent spawned which child, and how results propagated back up.
Because regressions are caught at the task level before deployment, prompt and model changes can be evaluated more aggressively.
Adding AgentOps tracing to every LLM call and tool call adds latency (typically 1-5 ms per call) and storage cost for trace data. For high-throughput applications this is non-trivial.
AgentOps platforms vary widely in their trace schemas and APIs. Migrating accumulated trace data between platforms is painful.
Agent traces capture what happened, but replaying a trace to reproduce a bug requires freezing tool outputs and model responses — which may not match reality on replay.
Agents that pass state purely through the context window leave no persistent state for AgentOps to inspect between calls. You must explicitly emit state snapshots.
AgentOps as a field is far younger than MLOps. Many capabilities — automatic anomaly detection for reasoning loops, agent-level SLOs — are still research problems.
AgentOps does not replace LLMOps. You still need model versioning, inference serving, prompt libraries, and benchmark suites. AgentOps is an additive layer, not a replacement.
LLMOps and AgentOps each operate on different units of work and at different granularities.

A prompt enters the inference API, a model version is selected from the registry, the response is returned, and the event (prompt, response, latency, token count, cost) is logged. Evaluation pipelines run nightly to compare the current model version against a benchmark suite.

When an agent run starts, a trace session is opened. Each reasoning step, LLM call, and tool invocation is recorded as a child span within that session. The session carries metadata: the task goal, the agent version, the user ID, and the plan the agent constructed. When the run completes (or fails), the session is closed and cost is attributed to it.

The two layers share the same underlying LLM call logs — AgentOps attaches session context to each LLM call that LLMOps already records. The session context links individual model calls into a coherent run-level story.

attrs.alt: LLMOps vs AgentOps: LLMOps handles model registry, prompt library, inference serving, cost metrics, and evaluation pipeline. AgentOps extends this with agent state manager, tool registry, memory store, step-level traces, and safety guardrails.

content.text: The architecture shows two complementary stacks. The LLMOps stack (left, blue) handles model-level concerns: registering and versioning model weights, storing prompt templates, serving the inference API, and collecting per-request metrics. The AgentOps stack (right, green) wraps the LLMOps layer and adds agent-session-level concerns. The amber arrow in the middle emphasizes that AgentOps does not replace LLMOps — it extends it.

your application is a pipeline of independent, single-shot LLM calls — document summarization, classification, extraction, or Q&A over a fixed context. Each call is self-contained and the failure mode is a bad response to a single prompt.
your application involves any of the following:
Agents that loop — calling the LLM multiple times to reason about the same task.
Tool use — the agent calls external APIs, databases, code interpreters, or browsers.
Memory — the agent stores and retrieves information across turns or across separate runs.
Planning — the agent constructs and follows a multi-step plan rather than responding immediately.
Multi-agent systems — one agent spawns or delegates to other agents.
Long-running tasks — agent runs that take minutes or hours rather than milliseconds.
In practice, most production agents need both: LLMOps for the model infrastructure and AgentOps for the session-level visibility that makes complex agent behavior debuggable.
The following steps add AgentOps instrumentation to an existing LLM application that already has LLMOps tooling in place.
Options include AgentOps.ai, LangSmith, Langfuse, and Weights and Biases Weave. Evaluate based on your LLM provider integration, data residency requirements, and pricing model.
Wrap your agent's main function with a session context that captures the task goal, user ID, agent version, and a unique run ID.
Emit span events for LLM calls.
Each call to the LLM should emit a child span with the prompt, model, temperature, token counts, and latency. Most platforms provide automatic instrumentation for OpenAI, Anthropic, and LangChain.
Emit span events for tool calls.
Each tool invocation should emit a child span with the tool name, arguments, result, and any errors. Do not log sensitive data (PII, credentials) in tool arguments.
Record state snapshots at decision points.
When the agent makes a planning decision or updates its memory, emit a custom event with a snapshot of the relevant state.
Attach tags like task_type, user_tier, or feature_flag to each session so you can filter traces and compare cost and quality across segments.
Before executing tool calls, run the arguments through a validation function that rejects or flags dangerous actions. Log the guardrail decision as a span event.
Create a test suite of representative agent tasks with known correct outcomes. Run this suite against every model or prompt change before deployment.
Setting Up AgentOps Tracing with the Anthropic SDK

import agentops
import anthropic

# Initialize AgentOps — this patches the Anthropic client automatically
agentops.init(api_key="your-agentops-api-key", default_tags=["agentic-ai", "prod"])

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_agent(task: str, user_id: str) -> str:
    """Run a simple single-agent loop with full AgentOps tracing."""
    with agentops.start_session(tags=["research-agent"], user_id=user_id) as session:
        session.record(agentops.Event(event_type="task_start", params={"task": task}))

        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
        final_response = None

        for step in range(10):  # safety limit: max 10 reasoning steps
            response = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
                max_tokens=1024,
                messages=messages,
            )
            content = response.content[0].text
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": content})

            # AgentOps auto-captures the Anthropic call above as a child span.
            session.record(agentops.Event(
                event_type="reasoning_step",
                params={"step": step, "stop_reason": response.stop_reason},
            ))

            if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                final_response = content
                break

        session.record(agentops.Event(event_type="task_end", params={"steps": step + 1}))
        return final_response or "Agent did not reach a conclusion"

import hashlib
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class PromptVersion:
    version_id: str
    template: str
    description: str
    created_at: str

class PromptRegistry:
    """Minimal LLMOps prompt registry with SHA-based versioning."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._store: dict[str, PromptVersion] = {}
        self._active: dict[str, str] = {}

    def register(self, name: str, template: str, description: str) -> str:
        content_hash = hashlib.sha256(template.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]
        version_id = f"{name}-{content_hash}"
        self._store[version_id] = PromptVersion(
            version_id=version_id,
            template=template,
            description=description,
            created_at=datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        )
        return version_id

    def activate(self, name: str, version_id: str) -> None:
        if version_id not in self._store:
            raise KeyError(f"Version {version_id} not found")
        self._active[name] = version_id

    def render(self, name: str, **kwargs) -> tuple[str, str]:
        """Returns (rendered_prompt, version_id) so callers can log the version."""
        version_id = self._active[name]
        pv = self._store[version_id]
        return pv.template.format(**kwargs), version_id


registry = PromptRegistry()

v1 = registry.register(
    name="summarize",
    template="Summarize the following in 3 sentences:\n\n{document}",
    description="Baseline summarization prompt",
)
v2 = registry.register(
    name="summarize",
    template=(
        "You are an expert technical writer. Summarize the following document "
        "in exactly 3 bullet points. Each bullet must be one sentence.\n\n{document}"
    ),
    description="Structured bullet-point summarization",
)

# A/B evaluation: run both versions against the same document, compare quality
document = "Agentic AI systems combine LLMs with tools and memory to complete complex tasks..."

for version in [v1, v2]:
    registry.activate("summarize", version)
    prompt, vid = registry.render("summarize", document=document)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    # Log: version_id=vid, tokens=response.usage, output=response.content[0].text
    print(f"Version {vid}: {response.content[0].text[:80]}...")

A legal research assistant agent is given the task "Identify all indemnification clauses in these three contracts and flag any that cap liability below $1M." The agent calls the LLM to plan the approach, then iteratively calls a document-search tool to retrieve relevant clauses, and finally calls the LLM to analyze and compare them. AgentOps traces the entire run; LLMOps handles the underlying model serving.
Applying only LLMOps — logging individual LLM calls without session context — makes it impossible to reconstruct what an agent did across a multi-step run.
Emitting only a single event per agent run (success or failure) without step-level spans makes root cause analysis impossible. Every LLM call and every tool call needs its own span.
Agents without a hard iteration limit can loop indefinitely on ambiguous tasks, driving up cost. Always set a maximum step count and emit an alert when it is reached.
Tool arguments frequently contain PII, API keys, or confidential document content. Scrub sensitive fields before they reach the AgentOps platform.
Setting LLMOps cost alerts at the request level does not catch a single agent run that calls the LLM 50 times. Implement per-session cost tracking with a hard abort when the budget is exceeded.
Conflating prompt regression with agent logic regression.

A benchmark drop at the LLMOps level may be caused by a model change, a prompt change, or a change in the tools the agent is given. Keep model evals, prompt evals, and end-to-end task evals as separate pipelines.

Agents that update long-term memory can accumulate stale or incorrect beliefs over time. AgentOps should track memory read and write events so you can audit what the agent believed at each decision point.
Separate model evals from task evals.
LLMOps evaluations should benchmark the model on isolated prompts. AgentOps evaluations should test full end-to-end agent tasks with realistic tool stubs.
Tag every span with consistent keys: agent_version, model_id, task_type, user_id, step_number. Inconsistent tagging makes dashboards meaningless.
Set per-session cost and step budgets.
Enforce hard limits in code, not just alerts. An agent that exceeds its budget should abort and return a partial result with an error, not continue silently.
Agent behavior is determined by the prompt, the tool definitions, the model, and the memory retrieval logic. Track all four as a versioned unit when rolling out changes.
After scrubbing PII, store the complete tool call input and output for each step. Partial logs make debugging nearly impossible.
Collect and curate traces of successful agent runs on representative tasks. Use these as regression baselines when upgrading the model or changing the prompt.
In production, you need the ability to terminate a running agent session immediately — for example, if guardrails detect that the agent is about to take a destructive action.

LLMOps infrastructure scales with request throughput. Standard techniques — horizontal scaling of inference servers, request batching, KV cache sharing — apply directly. The key LLMOps scaling metric is tokens per second per dollar.
AgentOps scaling is fundamentally different because agents are long-lived, stateful sessions rather than stateless requests:

High-concurrency agent deployments can generate thousands of span events per second. Use a write-optimized time-series or columnar store (ClickHouse, TimescaleDB) for AgentOps data — not a relational database designed for transactional workloads.
Emit spans asynchronously so instrumentation does not add latency to the critical path. Buffer events locally and flush in batches.
Agent memory stores (vector databases for semantic retrieval) must handle concurrent reads and writes from many agent sessions. Pre-partition by user or session and use read replicas for retrieval.

For tasks that can be parallelized (analyzing 100 documents simultaneously), spawn parallel agent runs and aggregate results. AgentOps must correlate spans across parallel sessions via a parent session ID.

Running a 500-task agent eval suite takes significant time if each task spawns a full agent loop. Cache deterministic tool outputs and use lighter evaluation models for subset evals during development.
AgentOps.ai (per-session cost), custom via span metadata
AgentOps action hooks, LangChain callbacks, custom validators
Task success rate, tool call accuracy
Task success rate, steps-to-completion, run cost
prompt injection (user input that hijacks the prompt), output filtering (blocking harmful content), and credential management for model API keys.

If the agent reads a web page or document that contains embedded instructions, the agent may execute malicious tool calls. Guardrails must inspect tool arguments before execution, not just filter LLM outputs.

Agents should operate on the principle of least privilege. Grant only the specific tool permissions needed for each task. An agent that can write to any file path is far more dangerous than one constrained to a specific directory.
In multi-tenant deployments, agent sessions from different users must be isolated at the memory store, tool execution, and trace storage layers. Cross-session memory contamination is a serious risk.
AgentOps trace data is often used for compliance and forensic purposes. Protect it with write-once storage and cryptographic integrity checks.
Rate limiting at the agent level.
A compromised account that triggers many agent runs can incur massive cost. Implement per-user and per-agent-type rate limits at the session layer, not just at the LLM API level.
When an agent run fails mid-execution, its partial state should be preserved in a dead-letter store for investigation and potential replay — not silently discarded.
covers model versioning, prompt management, inference serving, per-request cost and latency metrics, and benchmark evaluation. It treats the individual LLM call as its primary unit of work.

extends LLMOps by adding session-level tracing, tool call auditing, agent state management, memory store integration, and per-run cost attribution. It treats the multi-step agent run as its primary unit of work.
: every agent system needs LLMOps for the underlying model infrastructure, plus AgentOps for the higher-order session visibility that makes complex agent behavior debuggable and controllable.
every LLM call and every tool call
as a child span within an agent session. Logging only at the session level (success or failure) is insufficient for debugging multi-step failures.
in code, not just as monitoring alerts. Agents without hard limits can loop unexpectedly and incur catastrophic API costs.
, not just at the LLM output layer. Checking the text of a model response for dangerous content does not prevent prompt injection attacks that route through tool arguments.

— the prompt template, model ID, tool definitions, and memory retrieval logic together determine agent behavior. Changing any one component requires re-evaluating the full agent, not just the changed component.
